hub_subtitle: Inglês executivo para entrevistas nos EUA e apresentações de alto impacto

lesson.menu_title: Past Simple vs. Present Perfect -- Framing Your Story in an Interview
lesson.menu_desc: Choosing the right tense: past simple for what you did, present perfect for why it still matters -- 32 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 3 -- Past Simple vs. Present Perfect in Job Interviews
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Lucia Nishiyama Serra | Lesson 3 -- Past Simple vs. Present Perfect in Interviews

listenings.text: Let me tell you what I listen for when I interview a senior leader. Two things, really. First, the facts. What did you actually do? I want the past simple here. You led a team, you shipped a product, you closed a deal in a specific year. That is your record, and it needs to be clear. But facts alone do not win the offer. The second thing is the bridge. Why does that experience still matter, here, today, for this role? That is where the present perfect does the work. When a candidate says, I have carried that lesson into every project since, I lean in. Do not just list what you did ten years ago. Show me why it still lives in how you work now.

So, when they ask me to walk through my background, I try to do two things at once. I start with the facts, in the past. I joined the company in 2015. I led the security team through a very hard migration that year. We deployed a new framework across four countries, and we hit every deadline. Those are finished events, so I use the past simple. But then I connect it to now. Since that project, I have owned every rollout I touch. I have built a real track record of shipping on time, and I have never lost that sense of accountability. The past tells them what happened. The present perfect tells them who I am today. That is the difference between an archive and an argument.

extra_audio.text: In 2015, I joined the company as a security lead. That year, I led a difficult migration across four countries. We deployed the new framework and delivered every outcome on time. Since that project, I have owned every rollout I touch. And I have carried that sense of accountability into every role since.
